Yks asia mikä tosin itteä näin millenniaalina ihmetyttää ja ärsyttääkin, on se, että sitten totta tosiaan ihan kaikki kysytään Jodelista. Missä on lähikaupassa kananmunat, tarkeneeko ulkona hupparissa, missä on sen ja tuon paikan sisäänkäynti, mitä ostaa lahjaksi pylle (poikaystävä), mitä tarkoittaa rsvp, miten työpaikalla toimii asia x, mitä tilais woltista tai voiko pöydälle unohtunutta ruokaa syödä. Kanavia löytyy asunnonostajista taitoluisteluun, keskiluokasta kielipoliiseihin, työpaikkavaroituksista tiktokjuttuihin, blogeihin, viesintään, aspakanaviin, tv-ohjelmiin, laihdutuslääkkeistä pistoshoitoihin, alennuskoodeihin, kaupunginosiin, kauneuteen ja terveyteen, urheiluun, perheeseen ja vauvakanaviin, oppilaitoksiin, museokorttiin, you name it. document.title: Create your own AI Sales Agent by Michael Lovegrove

json.text:
You can build an AI agent in 10 minutes.

(But 99% of people will never attempt it)

I've built multiple AI agents for Snowballn' and Sollo.
And I'm still shocked at how easy it is.

If you can write a basic checklist, you can build an agent.

One that will genuinely save you (and your team!) 100s of hours.

Here's exactly how to build one 👇

1. Pick One Boring Job
→ Choose one painful workflow you repeat weekly.
→ Define success in one sentence: "Given X, the agent should output Y so that Z happens."

2. Map The Steps Like A SOP
→ Turn that job into 4 to 7 clear steps.
→ Write it as: Input → Actions → Decision → Output.

3. Choose Your Agent Platform
→ No/low code: OpenAI Agent Builder, Zapier, Make, n8n.
→ Dev-friendly: LangChain, LangGraph, OpenAI Agents SDK, CrewAI.

4. Define Inputs, Outputs, And Tools
→ Treat the agent like an API, not a vague chatbot.
→ Specify what fields are required. Define outputs as JSON or fixed templates.

5. Give The Agent A Job Description
→ Write a clear system prompt covering role, boundaries, style, and 1-2 example conversations.

6. Add Memory And Context
→ Pass recent messages so it stays on topic. Store key decisions. 
→ Connect knowledge memory.

7. Add Guardrails And Human Checks
→ Mark high-risk actions that always need approval.
→ Log every tool call so you can audit behaviour.

8. Wrap It In A Simple Interface
→ One field for inputs. A clear "Run agent" button. A result panel.

9. Test On 5 Real Tasks
→ Watch which tools it calls and in what order.
→ Score correctness, steps taken, and time saved.

Those that adopt AI correctly now will be too far ahead to catch later.

If you want to be one of them, you need to start automating tedious work (the stuff that you don't need a human for...)


Want the exact templates, frameworks and frameworks to feed your first agent?

📍Grab free access to them in the Snowballn' Sales Vault: https://lnkd.in/gkBnZFpN

document.title: What sales ACTUALLY is by Michael Lovegrove

json.text:
97% of people think sales is convincing someone to buy...

It's not.

Sales isn't about persuasion.

It's about orchestration.
____________________
⇢ FREE RESOURCE: 10 FREE sales resources
https://lnkd.in/gkBnZFpN
(that will help you get leads and close deals)
---------------------------------

If you're still running demos like it's 2019, relying on marketing to fill your pipeline, or thinking "more features" will close deals...

You're being left behind.

Here's what sales actually is in 2026...

1. Lead Generation

You should not depend on marketing to produce leads. You need to know how to build pipeline on your own.

Consistency beats perfection.


2. Problem Finding

Most people tell you what they want. 
Features, solutions, specific requirements.

Sales job is to dig deeper.
Find WHY they want what they want.


3. Challenging Buyers

You're not an order taker.
You're changing how they see the problem. Potentially showing them the problem exists in the first place.

Make doing nothing riskier than changing.


4. Facilitating change

Sales is about change. 
And people do not like change. 

Your job is to help your buyer navigate this change.

Make their job easy.


5. Negotiation

It's got to be a fair exchange.
Information and value flowing both ways.
Never give without getting. 

Trade value for value. 
Always.


6. Stamina

Being able to get punched in the face and keep going.
 
You will get ignored, ghosted, rejected.

The best keep going.

---

Sales is no linear.

Deals don't close in straight lines. 
They loop, stall, and restart.

It's normal.

If you're treating sales like a pitch contest instead of a facilitation exercise, you're:

→ Burning through leads without converting
→ Losing deals in the negotiation phase
→ Wondering why "interested" never turns into "closed"

Then you probably want to access the Snowballn' Sales Vault: https://lnkd.in/gkBnZFpN

json.text:
CHALLENGER IS A MINDSET

Recent Prisma (Finland’s biggest hypermarket) campaign “Big selection. Little prices.” has been getting deservedly positive feedback. 

Not surprisingly: it uses humour (still the most effective emotional shortcut) and stays rigorously consistent with the brand platform built earlier. Disciplined advertising at its finest. 

But the most interesting part is this:
Nothing is more powerful than a market leader behaving like a challenger.

The challenger position is usually not for the faint-hearted. You have to pick an enemy. And that enemy is typically bigger, richer, and has more lawyers than you.

When a market leader like Prisma pulls it off, something Machiavellian happens: You keep the scale and leverage of the market leader, but you borrow the relatability, hunger, and agility of a challenger.
A wolf in sheep’s clothing, if you will.

It may also signal something else: a void of real challenger brands in the category. Either they don’t realise they’re challengers, or they lack the courage to act like one.

Market leaders win when the category grows.
Challengers only grow by taking market share from competitors.
Which means, inconveniently, that peace is not an option.
Fight is.

#branding #strategy #challengerbrands #marketing #positioning

json.text:
The anxiety of building AI agents is real.
Here is my no-anxiety roadmap. ⬇️

I have been working with AI systems for a long time, across many waves of models, frameworks, and “next big things”.

And one pattern keeps repeating.

Every week, building and teaching AI agents, I see a quiet pressure showing up again and again.

Every morning there is a new framework.
A new “revolutionary” model.
And someone confidently declaring that what you built last month is already obsolete.

Not beginners.
Capable engineers.

Here is what shows up most often:
Imposter syndrome amplified.
Not because people lack skill, but because the goalposts keep moving.

Decision paralysis.
LangGraph or CrewAI?
OpenAI SDK or PydanticAI?
GPT, Claude, or Gemini?

So much time is lost debating tools that are, in practice, far more similar than different.

Most agent frameworks are just different libraries around the same ideas:
🔹 define a role,
🔹 structure inputs and outputs,
🔹 add reasoning,
🔹 call tools,
🔹 store memory,
🔹 evaluate results.

Same with models.
GPT, Claude, Gemini. Comparable capabilities. Slight trade-offs.

The real mistake is thinking you must pick “the right one” before you can start.

You do not.

🔹 Start with one.
🔹 Build something real.
🔹 Then expand.
Switching later is much easier than people think.

The hype vs reality gap adds pressure too.
Marketing promises magic.
Real systems deal with token limits, hallucinations, edge cases, evaluation, and failures.

And there is isolation.
Most people around you do not really understand why something “working” is not the same as something being reliable.

What is interesting is this:

The anxiety does not come from AI moving fast.

It comes from treating AI agents as tools instead of systems.

Once that mental model shifts, things calm down.

When you look at agents through stable building blocks:
→  role and goal
→  structured inputs and outputs
→  reasoning loops
→  tool use
→  memory
→  evaluation
Framework churn becomes noise.

This is also why recent research matters so much.
We are starting to treat LLMs less like text generators and more like systems that reason, check themselves, and refine explanations. Almost like scientists, not autocomplete engines.

Same principle.

Structure first. Tools second.

In practice, this consistently helps builders:

Finish one agent instead of five half-built ones.
Pick one framework and one model and go deep.
Expand later when you understand the trade-offs.
Measure success by solved problems, not novelty.

AI is moving fast.
But the fundamentals of building reliable systems move much slower.

Haluaisinko rekrytoida pahimpien kilpailijoidemme parhaimmat työntekijät? 

Nyt kun avasimme juuri (taas) kaksi työpaikkaa, tulin pohtineeksi: haluaisinko rekrytoida juuri näiden pahimpien kilpailijoidemme parhaat tyypit? 

Jos voisi siis valita:

1️⃣ Hyvä tyyppi ''jostain''
2️⃣ Yhtä hyvä tyyppi nimenomaan pahalta kilpailijaltamme

Hankala valinta, koska yllättävää kyllä, ajattelen: ihan sama. 

Mutta jos olisi ihanihan pakko valita, ehkä kakkonen. Miksi? Pääosin ei-siksi, että haluaisin kilpailijallemme pahaa. Mutta on myös totta, että tuskin menettäisin yöuniani sen vuoksi, että olen aiheuttanut harmaita hiuksia kilpailijallemme. 

Erikoinen rekrypostaus. Noh, kuitenkin:

1. Työpaikkoja auki (projektipäällikkö ja pääkehittäjä)
2. Ihan sama mistä tulet, kunhan olet hyvä ja hyvä tyyppi
3. Maksamme palkkaa vähintään vähän enemmän, kuin nyt saat

Hauskaa viikkoa!

json.text:
Iso firma ja pieni firma kohtaavat AI:n eri tavalla. Molemmat voivat hyötyä, mutta haasteet ovat erilsisia.

Isossa firmassa ongelma on byrokratia. IT-osasto haluaa tietoturva-arvioinnin. Legal haluaa sopimusläpikäynnin. Procurement haluaa kilpailutuksen. Kuukausia myöhemmin: "Voidaanko nyt aloittaa pilotti?"

Pienessä firmassa ongelma on resurssit. Kuka ehtii opetella uuden työkalun? Kuka rakentaa automaatiot? Kuka ylläpitää niitä? Usein vastaus on: sama tyyppi joka tekee kaiken muunkin.

Mutta molemmilla on myös etuja.

Iso firma: dataa on paljon, budjettia on, ja kun päätös syntyy, skaalaus onnistuu. Pieni firma: päätökset syntyvät nopeasti, kokeilukulttuuri on luontevaa, ja koko tiimi näkee hyödyt heti.

Parhaat tulokset syntyy kun iso firma ajattelee kuin pieni.
Pienet tiimit, nopeat kokeilut, vähemmän PowerPointteja.

Ja kun pieni firma ajattelee kuin iso. Dokumentoi prosessit, mieti skaalautuvuutta, älä rakenna vain yhden ihmisen varaan.

Haaste on erilainen, mutta ratkaisu on sama: aloita pienestä ja laajenna toimivasta.

comments.text:
Here’s a way to understand how AI can sound intelligent while still being just a computer.

Put about thirty ordinary words in a bucket. Try making 8-word sentences by picking eight words at random. Almost every result looks like noise, even though all the words are real.

This is because randomness overwhelms structure. Sentence-like word orders are extremely rare compared to all possible orders. Random picking almost never produces language.

Now change the bucket. Add multiple copies of glue words like “the”, “is”, “to”, “and”, “I”. Keep selecting randomly. Very quickly you will get plausible 8-word sentences such as:
“The quiet clock decided to apologize for yesterday.”

With just that adjustment, fluency appears without meaning. Many random picks now look like sentences. They sound right but often say little.

Adding word copies in this example is equivalent to training in AI: repeated exposure increases likelihood. No understanding was added, only weighting. This is how fluency appears before meaning.

As models get larger, sentences become longer and stories improve, but the computer still does not understand anything. Humans supply the understanding when we read the response to the prompt.

json.text:
AI tekee virheitä ja hallusinoi. Tämä ei ole bugi, se on ominaisuus.
Kysymys ei ole miten estät virheet. Kysymys on miten hallitset niitä.

Ensimmäinen taso: havaitseminen.
Miten tiedät kun AI tekee virheen? Jos luotat sokeasti, olet pulassa. Rakenna tarkistuspisteet. Ihmissilmä kriittisissä kohdissa.

Toinen taso: rajoittaminen.
Mitä AI saa tehdä itse, missä tarvitaan hyväksyntä? Liukuva skaala: matalan riskin tehtävät automaattisesti, korkean riskin tehtävät ihmisen kautta.

Kolmas taso: korjaaminen.

json.text:
Tailwindin suosio on räjähtänyt käsiin, mutta ajanut samalla taustalla olevan yhtiön talousvaikeuksiin 👀

Tailwind on siis semmonen tekninen kirjasto, jota käytetään verkkopalveluiden tyylien määrittelyyn. Eli sen avulla päätetään, miltä sovellus näyttää. Tekoälymallit suosii Tailwindiä ja se onkin räjäyttänyt suosion uusiin sfääreihin.

Mutta.

Tailwind tarjoaa myös kaupallisia palveluita. Heidän bisnesidea on ollut se, että ihmiset lukee heidän dokumentaatiotaan ja he voi samalla myydä omia palveluitaan.

Vaan kukaan ei enää lue sitä dokumentaatiota. Tekoäly hoitaa. Verkkoliikenne on tippunut 40% ja liikevaihto 80%. Tailwind joutui juuri irtisanomaan 75% henkilöstöstä (eli siis kolme tyyppiä).

Mielenkiintoista. Suosio räjähtää, tulot romahtaa. 

Tää on mun mielestä osoitus siitä, että tekoäly pakottaa yllättävätkin tahot miettimään bisnesmallejaan uudelleen.

Kaikissa noissa malleissa on omat haasteensa ja luottavat enemmän tai vähemmän muiden firmojen hyväntahtoisuuteen.

𝗦𝗽𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝘀 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗮𝗱𝘃𝗮𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗴𝗲 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗲𝗿𝗮 𝗼𝗳 𝗔𝗜.

But most companies don't see improvements. 

They buy Copilot licenses. Give them to developers. And expect results.

It doesn't work.

Because the process remains the same.

That's why we created the 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗕𝗹𝘂𝗲𝗽𝗿𝗶𝗻𝘁.

Whether you're:
• Accelerating existing product delivery
• Modernizing legacy systems
• Building something new

This framework applies.

───

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺 𝗶𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗼𝗳𝗳𝘀.

A product requirement written with AI gets manually copied into architecture docs.
Then copied again into tickets.
Then interpreted by developers who never saw the original.

Each handoff loses information. Each handoff adds delay.

───

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝗶𝘅: 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗰𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗿𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀.

Instead of isolated AI tools, deploy AI agents that pass work from phase to phase with full context.

𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗽𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗽𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸:

1/ Context first: create specs, architecture, and design before coding
2/ Context compounds: each phase builds on validated artifacts from the previous one
3/ Agent handover at every step
4/ Spec-driven development with granular task decomposition
5/ Human approval gates between phases

───

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝗵𝗼𝘄 𝗶𝘁 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀 𝗮𝗰𝗿𝗼𝘀𝘀 𝗳𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗽𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲𝘀:

𝗗𝗘𝗙𝗜𝗡𝗘: 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝘄𝗲 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱?
The Product Manager Agent reads your customer feedback, research, and existing docs. It writes the requirements and user stories. Then pushes them directly into your issue tracker.

𝗗𝗘𝗦𝗜𝗚𝗡: 𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗶𝘁 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸?
The Architect Agent picks up those requirements automatically. It already knows your codebase structure. It writes the technical spec.

𝗕𝗨𝗜𝗟𝗗: 𝗠𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝗶𝘁 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹.
The Developer Agent reads the spec and the designs. Writes the code. Creates the pull request.

The QA Agent reads everything that came before (requirements, designs, code) then writes the tests. Finds the bugs.

───

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀:

Each agent passes forward the artifact, the context, and the acceptance criteria.
The next agent starts with full information.
No handoff meetings. No lost context.

𝗥𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁𝘀: 30% to 70% productivity gains depending on the phase.
On average, our clients see a 𝟮.𝟮𝘅 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗲.

───

𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗲𝘀 𝗺𝗶𝘀𝘀:

You can't build this by optimizing development alone.

Speed isn't a dev problem. It's an end-to-end problem.

The bottleneck might be in product. Or design. Or QA. Or deployment approvals.

You'll never see it if each department optimizes in isolation.

Where does work wait longest in your organization?

As a data analyst, this reads like a throughput problem masquerading as a tooling problem. When you map product development as a value stream, the waste is obvious: rework, waiting time, context decay, and interpretation variance at every handoff. No amount of Copilot licenses fixes a broken flow. Most organizations optimize local efficiency (dev velocity, story points, sprint output) while ignoring system throughput. The result is predictable: utilization goes up, lead time stays flat.
What you’re describing is essentially constraint-aware, artifact-driven delivery:
 • Preserve information entropy across stages
 • Reduce queue time between phases
 • Enforce explicit acceptance criteria as control variables
 • Measure cycle time end-to-end, not per team
That’s how you get real speed by collapsing latency, not by pushing developers harder. If leaders actually instrumented their process, they’d discover the longest wait isn’t in code. It’s approvals, clarification loops, and translation layers. Speed is not a productivity metric. It’s a systems metric.
And most systems are flying blind.

How to explain AI coding to your leadership.
The wrong approach leads to failed projects.
(yet happens all the time)

For example, you can't ship an AI-generated demo.
Even if the CEO wants it in production immediately.

AI coding tools went from experiment to everyday use.
The question is: which approach fits which situation?

Here is the breakdown:

1/ 𝗩𝗶𝗯𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴

Think of it as "code without coding."

You describe what you want in plain English. AI writes the code.

Programming is no longer reserved for trained professionals.

When to use:
→ Testing product ideas before major investment
→ Demos for stakeholders
→ Business teams prototyping without developers

Don't use for: Production systems or mission-critical applications.

ROI: Prove value before investing.

Tools: Lovable, Bolt, Replit

2/ 𝗔𝗜-𝗔𝘀𝘀𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁

Developer productivity with complete control.

When to use:
→ Daily development work
→ Writing repetitive code
→ Improving code quality

ROI: 20-25% individual developers' productivity.

Tools: Cursor, Antigravity

They do "context engineering": orchestrating multiple AI calls while you stay in control.

3/ 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁

Autonomous execution of complex tasks.

Claude Code showed what an LLM Agent looks like: tool use + reasoning in a loop for extended problem solving.

You define the desired outcome. AI plans, codes, and tests.

When to use:
→ Migrating legacy systems
→ Large-scale code updates
→ Multi-step development work

ROI: 
• Accelerating existing product delivery 
• Modernizing legacy systems
• Bringing a new product to the market -- FAST

Tools: Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex

json.text:
You're using the wrong ChatGPT tone.

And it's costing you 20+ minutes of editing every time.

What's happening:

You use whatever tone is already selected.
Get back something that sounds off.
Then spend 20 minutes rewriting it.

What you're missing:

ChatGPT has 8 different tones.
Each changes structure, length, and style.
Wrong tone = generic output.
Right tone = nearly perfect first draft.

The 8 tones:

→ DEFAULT 
When: Everyday questions, mixed audiences 
What you get: Balanced, neutral, safe 
Use for: First drafts when you're not sure yet

→ PROFESSIONAL
When: Writing for clients, stakeholders, documentation 
What you get: Credible, polished, formal 
Use for: Business emails, reports, LinkedIn authority posts

→ FRIENDLY 
When: Teaching beginners, explaining complex stuff simply What you get: Approachable, clear, warm 
Use for: Tutorials, FAQs, guides

→ CANDID 
When: Giving honest feedback, career advice, reality checks 
What you get: Direct, no-sugarcoating, encouraging 
Use for: Personal branding posts, mentorship content

→ QUIRKY 
When: Creating engaging social content, hooks 
What you get: Playful, creative, attention-grabbing 
Use for: Social posts, scripts, emails with personality

→ EFFICIENT 
When: You need the answer fast, no fluff 
What you get: Short, clear, actionable 
Use for: Checklists, how-tos, step-by-step guides

→ NERDY 
When: Technical audience that wants depth 
What you get: Detailed explanations, shows the mechanics 
Use for: Engineering docs, AI architecture, deep dives

→ CYNICAL 
When: Calling out hype, challenging bad advice 
What you get: Sharp, skeptical, opinionated 
Use for: Trend analysis, controversial takes (use carefully)

The difference:

Same email prompt. Three tones:
Default: "I wanted to follow up regarding the concerns you raised..."
Professional: "Thank you for raising these questions. Here's what's happening..."
Friendly: "Hey! I saw your questions and wanted to clear things up..."
One sounds stiff. One sounds corporate. One sounds human.

Why it matters:

Tone controls sentence length, word complexity, context depth, and whether it uses examples.
Change the tone, change everything.

Do this now:

Before your next prompt:

→ Who's reading this?
→ What's the context?
→ Should this sound formal or human?

Pick the matching tone.

Your editing time drops by half.

💬 Which tone do you overuse?

📌 Save this, you'll reference it constantly.

Right tone = better first draft. Wrong tone = 20 minutes of fixing.

→ Repost so others stop wasting time editing outputs.

json.text:
This might be the most important AI video you’ll see in 2026.

Your data.
Your voice.
Your face.

All can now be copied in seconds.

And it’s already happening quietly, at scale.

AI doesn’t need your password.
It learns from what you post.

Every photo. Every video. Every story.
Training systems to look, sound, and reason like you.

The uncomfortable truth?
Once it learns you, it can simulate you.

Fear isn’t the answer.
Awareness is.

Here’s a practical AI safety playbook 👇

For everyday users
→ Pause before you post. Your digital shadow compounds
→ Blur children’s faces and avoid real-time location tags
→ Don’t publish long, clean voice samples
→ Add watermarks or on-screen text to original content

For business users
→ Keep sensitive work off public AI tools
→ Share frameworks, not personal identifiers
→ Train teams on deepfakes and data misuse
→ Diversify platforms. Don’t anchor identity to one feed

AI isn’t waiting for consent.
It’s already learning from us.

The real question isn’t if you’ll be copied.
It’s which version of you gets learned.

resharedPost.text:
Dentsu Can't Give Away a $4.5bn Business. That Should Terrify You.

Dentsu's attempt to sell its international operations has collapsed. Apollo walked. Trade buyers walked. Only Bain Capital remains, "with significant reservations."

This is a business generating over $4.5 billion in net revenues.

Nobody wants to buy a $4.5 billion advertising business. Not at any price that makes sense.

This isn't a Dentsu problem. This is an industry problem.

Potential buyers deemed it "too difficult to turn around, given the downward trend."

Translation: We looked at the business model and couldn't see how to make it work.

That's not management failure. That's category failure.

Here's what's actually happening:

For decades, holding companies scaled by aggregating clients, capabilities, and talent. The model was: get bigger, negotiate better rates, spread costs.

But scale only works if there's something scarce to scale.

What happens when AI makes the scalable bits free?

When ChatGPT can write briefs, generate concepts, and optimise media—the value of having 100,000 people doing those things efficiently doesn't compound. It evaporates.

The brutal maths: Dentsu's cutting 3,400 jobs. One shareholder cites an "outdated approach to a market undergoing fundamental upheaval."

They're right. But the "outdated approach" isn't bad management. It's the entire industrial model of advertising—where value came from efficiently executing repeatable processes at scale.

AI doesn't disrupt that model. AI makes it worthless.

The question nobody wants to ask: If a profitable $4.5bn advertising business can't be sold, what exactly are we all worth?

The uncomfortable answer: Only the bits AI can't do. Judgment, taste, strategic contrarianism, and the ability to say "this entire approach is wrong."

Everything else isn't valuable anymore. It's cost.

Meanwhile: Dentsu faces "stiffer competition from the IPG-Omnicom merger" and "growing dominance of Publicis."

So the solution is... get bigger? Merge with other struggling networks? Create even larger operations doing things AI is making obsolete?

That's not strategy. That's arranging deck chairs efficiently.

The real problem: We've spent 15 years optimising for scale, efficiency, and process. We've become very good at things that no longer matter.

And now we're shocked nobody wants to buy our ability to do them at impressive scale.

Dentsu shareholders vote on leadership in March. But changing the CEO won't fix this. The model is broken.

Not because Dentsu did anything wrong. Because they did everything right—for a world that no longer exists.

When a $4.5bn business can't find a buyer, that's not a valuation problem. 

That's existential.

comments.text:
Great write up.

If I were looking at Dentsu, I wouldn’t try to tweak it. I’d break it up.

I'd be honest about which parts of the group are actually winning clients at good margins and which parts are just legacy drag. Ring-fence the winners and run them as growth businesses. Everything else needs a hard reset.

That means real restructuring, walking away from unprofitable clients, and rebuilding the company around growth strategy rather than selling time. Fewer people, senior partners focused on business growth and strategy at the centre of the business. Incentivised on outcomes, not utilisation. Much closer to private equity thinking than agency thinking.

The commercial model has to change. Time and materials is broken at this scale. Agencies only win again when they share in the value they create. AI should be used to fundamentally reinvent how the business runs, not just to shave a bit of cost.

Short-term pain would be unavoidable but if you’re willing to trade revenue for quality, growth and automation, the multiple growth will drive shareholder value. 

This isn’t a one year fix, more like a five year reset.

 Shareholders would need patience which I imagine is an impossible sell

comments.text:
The diagnosis is sharp – but the conclusion is still too final.
AI makes execution cheap, not thinking obsolete.

Scaling through headcount no longer works.

Scaling through judgement, accountability and impact still does.

What we’re seeing at Dentsu isn’t just a valuation issue – it’s a value-creation challenge.
Doing the right things at scale stopped being enough.

But this isn’t unfixable. Examples across the market – from MediaPlus to parts of Publicis – show that agency models can work when they shift from delivery at scale to ownership, seniority and outcome-driven models.

AI replaces juniors. It strengthens seniors.

This isn’t category death. It’s a re-pricing of value – and a test of who can actually transform.
